name: HL7 FHIR API
what_it_does: HL7 FHIR (Fast Healthcare Interoperability Resources) is the global standard REST API for electronic health records. Defines how clinical data (patients, medications, observations, conditions) is structured and exchanged across healthcare systems.
use_cases: Reading patient health records from EHR systems (Epic, Cerner, Allscripts) for clinical AI
Querying medication lists, allergies, and diagnoses for care coordination agents
Integrating health data from multiple providers into unified patient views
Clinical decision support by accessing structured patient history
Building patient-facing health apps that access their own records (SMART on FHIR)
not_for: Billing and revenue cycle management (use X12 EDI for claims)
Imaging storage (DICOM for medical imaging, not FHIR)
Lab result ordering (may require HL7 v2 LIS integration)
Non-clinical administrative data outside the FHIR resource model
best_when: An agent needs to access or exchange structured clinical health data across EHR systems in a standardized way, especially in the US under ONC 21st Century Cures Act mandates.
avoid_when: You need claims/billing data, medical imaging, or are not working in the healthcare domain.

auth.notes: SMART on FHIR is the standard auth framework — OAuth2 with FHIR-specific scopes (patient/Patient.read, user/Observation.read, etc.). Each EHR vendor implements SMART on FHIR differently. Client credentials for backend agents, authorization code for patient-facing apps.

pricing.free_tier_limits: Open-source FHIR server (HAPI FHIR) free to self-host; public test servers available
pricing.paid_tiers: Hosted FHIR servers (Azure Health Data Services, AWS HealthLake) vary by storage and requests
pricing.notes: FHIR is a standard, not a product. Costs depend on the EHR system or FHIR server vendor. Cloud FHIR services start at ~$0.05-0.25 per 1000 requests. EHR vendor access may have separate licensing.

performance.rate_limits: Varies by server implementation; typically 100-1000 requests/minute

agent_readiness.error_message_notes: FHIR uses OperationOutcome resources for errors with issue.severity, issue.code, and diagnostics. Well-standardized format. Quality varies by EHR implementation — some vendors have excellent messages, others are terse.
agent_readiness.idempotency_notes: FHIR PUT with known ID is idempotent (upsert). POST creates new resource without idempotency. Conditional creates (If-None-Exist header) provide idempotent creation patterns.
known_agent_gotchas: Each EHR vendor implements FHIR differently — same query may work on Epic but not Cerner
SMART on FHIR OAuth2 flows differ between patient-facing and backend service apps
Resource profiles and extensions vary by vendor — generic FHIR code may need vendor-specific tuning
Patient consent and access controls are complex — agents must respect authorization grants
FHIR R4 vs STU3 vs R5 version differences — check which version the EHR supports
Searching FHIR requires knowledge of search parameters — not all parameters supported by all servers
PHI handling requires HIPAA BAA — agents must ensure data handling compliance
Bundle transactions for multi-resource writes have complex interdependency rules
